era_context: The mid-17th century: England under Oliver Cromwell's Commonwealth; the Thirty Years' War recently concluded (1648); the Ottoman Empire under Mehmed IV; the Qing Dynasty consolidating control over China; the Mughal Empire under Shah Jahan (Aurangzeb will become emperor in 1655). Scientific revolution accelerates; colonial expansion by European powers continues in the Americas and Asia.

events.title: Beginning of the English Western Design expedition
events.description: Oliver Cromwell dispatches a major naval and military expedition under Admiral William Penn and General Robert Venables to the Caribbean as part of his 'Western Design'—a strategic initiative to expand English colonial power and weaken Spanish hegemony in the Americas. The expedition sets sail in late 1654 with the initial objective of capturing Hispaniola. Although the assault on Hispaniola fails, the expedition successfully captures Jamaica in May 1655, establishing a major English colonial foothold in the Caribbean. The Western Design represents Cromwell's ambition to create a global English empire and reflects the growing naval and military capacity of the Commonwealth.

sources.name: English naval records and contemporary accounts
Historiographic consensus on English colonial expansion

events.certainty_note: The expedition's dispatch and objectives are well-documented in English records.
cross_references: 1660-001 (Restoration of Charles II; Jamaica remains English)

events.title: Treaty of Westminster between England and the Dutch Republic
events.description: England and the Dutch Republic sign the Treaty of Westminster, ending the First Anglo-Dutch War (1652–1654). The treaty recognises English naval power and colonial expansion, with the Dutch ceding some territorial concessions and accepting English dominance in certain colonial regions. The treaty reflects the shift in European power dynamics, with England emerging as a major naval power under Cromwell. The First Anglo-Dutch War was fought primarily over trade, colonial competition, and naval supremacy, and the English victory established the Commonwealth as a formidable maritime power.
events.certainty_note: The treaty date and terms are well-documented in diplomatic records.
cross_references: 1660-001 (Restoration of Charles II; treaty remains in effect)

events.title: Qing forces capture Nanjing from Ming loyalists
events.description: Qing forces, under the direction of the Kangxi Emperor's regents, capture the strategic city of Nanjing from Ming loyalist forces. This represents a major step in the Qing consolidation of control over southern China. Nanjing's capture eliminates a significant centre of Ming resistance and demonstrates the Qing's military superiority. By 1654, the Qing had effectively secured most of mainland China, though coastal piracy and the Zheng family's resistance in Taiwan and Fujian persisted until the 1680s.

sources.name: Qing imperial records and the Kangxi Emperor's reign records

events.certainty_note: The capture of Nanjing is well-documented in Qing imperial records.
cross_references: 1683-001 (Qing conquest of Taiwan, final Ming loyalist stronghold)

events.title: Shabbetai Zevi proclaimed as the Messiah
events.description: Shabbetai Zevi, a Jewish mystic and self-proclaimed Messiah, gains widespread following among Jewish communities throughout the Ottoman Empire and beyond. His movement, which peaked in 1654–1656, attracted thousands of followers who believed he was the long-awaited Messiah. The movement caused significant social upheaval within Jewish communities, with some followers abandoning their livelihoods and families to follow Zevi. In 1666, Zevi was arrested by Ottoman authorities and forced to convert to Islam, which led to the collapse of the movement, though some followers (the Dönmeh) continued to practise a syncretic form of Judaism and Islam. The Shabbetean movement represents a significant episode in Jewish history and demonstrates the religious ferment of the period.
key_figures: Nathan of Gaza (Zevi's prophet and promoter)
events.certainty_note: The date of Zevi's proclamation as Messiah is traditionally dated to 1654, though some sources place it slightly earlier or later. The movement's peak is well-attested.
cross_references: 1656-004 (Menasseh ben Israel's petition to Cromwell, contemporary Jewish activity)

events.title: Blaise Pascal's conversion experience (Night of Fire)
events.description: French mathematician and physicist Blaise Pascal experiences a profound religious conversion, often referred to as the 'Night of Fire' (Nuit de Feu), which he records in a brief, intensely personal note. This experience marks a turning point in Pascal's life, leading him to withdraw from scientific pursuits and devote himself to religious and philosophical reflection. The conversion experience is dated to November 23, 1654, and is commemorated in Pascal's 'Memorial,' a small parchment he carried with him for the rest of his life. This event sets the stage for Pascal's later writing of the Pensées and his engagement with Jansenism, a reform movement within Catholicism.

sources.name: Blaise Pascal's Memorial (personal record of the conversion experience)

events.certainty_note: The date of Pascal's conversion experience is recorded in his Memorial and confirmed by biographical sources.

events.title: Kangxi Emperor begins personal rule of Qing Dynasty
events.description: The Kangxi Emperor, who ascended to the throne in 1661 as a child, begins his personal rule of the Qing Dynasty in 1654 (though some sources date his effective assumption of power to 1667, when he dismissed the regents). The Kangxi Emperor is one of the most significant rulers in Chinese history, presiding over a period of territorial expansion, cultural flourishing, and administrative reform. His reign (1661–1722) is marked by the consolidation of Qing control over China, the conquest of Taiwan, and the expansion of Qing influence into Central Asia. The Kangxi Emperor's policies and achievements set the stage for the Qing Dynasty's emergence as one of the world's great powers.

sources.name: Qing imperial records and the Kangxi Emperor's reign records

events.certainty_note: The Kangxi Emperor's ascension to the throne is dated to 1661, but the date of his assumption of personal rule is disputed. Some sources place it in 1654, others in 1667. The historiographic consensus leans toward 1667 as the date when he effectively dismissed the regents.
cross_references: 1667-001 (Kangxi Emperor dismisses regents and assumes full personal rule)

disconfirming_evidence: The Treaty of Westminster is sometimes presented as a clear English victory, but modern scholarship recognises that the treaty was a compromise that reflected the exhaustion of both powers after the First Anglo-Dutch War. The Dutch retained significant colonial and naval power despite the treaty. The Qing capture of Nanjing is sometimes portrayed as the final consolidation of Qing power, but Ming loyalist resistance, particularly under the Zheng family, persisted for nearly three decades. Shabbetai Zevi's movement is sometimes characterised as a marginal phenomenon, but it actually attracted tens of thousands of followers and caused significant social upheaval within Jewish communities. The dating of Kangxi's assumption of personal rule is disputed; while some sources place it in 1654, the historiographic consensus now favours 1667 as the date when he effectively dismissed the regents and assumed full control.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1654 is rich for European, Ottoman, and East Asian sources. English records are detailed due to Commonwealth bureaucracy. Dutch records provide perspective on Anglo-Dutch relations. Jewish sources on the Shabbetean movement are abundant but often reflect the perspectives of different communities and factions. Chinese sources are voluminous but reflect Qing-era biases. Scientific and philosophical records (Pascal) are well-preserved in personal correspondence and manuscripts. The historical record is shaped by the dominance of elite, state-level, and intellectual sources; economic, social, and cultural history of non-elite populations is underrepresented. Caribbean indigenous perspectives are largely absent from surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: The Western Design expedition dispatched in 1654 resulted in the conquest of Jamaica in 1655.
The Treaty of Westminster remained in effect after the Restoration of Charles II in 1660.
The Qing capture of Nanjing in 1654 was part of the broader consolidation that culminated in the conquest of Taiwan in 1683.
Shabbetai Zevi's proclamation as Messiah in 1654 led to his forced conversion to Islam in 1666.
Pascal's conversion experience in 1654 led to his writing of the Pensées beginning in 1655.
The Kangxi Emperor's early rule (1654 or 1661) culminated in his assumption of full personal rule in 1667.
